ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a generic milk tea entry; no specific Thai iced milk tea with popping boba entry exists. Closest defensible match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKP covers tea-based beverages with milk; no specific Thai iced milk tea variant with popping boba exists in FoodEx2 as of 2024. Approximate match used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code for Thai iced milk tea with honey popping boba. Not assigned to avoid fabrication.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 400 g (one full cup), which is the typical portion size for a Thai iced milk tea sold at a bubble tea or milk tea outlet in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Beverages, tea, black, brewed, prepared with tap water; and Beverages, tea, Thai iced tea (estimated composite); supplemented with Singapore HPB Nutrient Composition of Food for condensed milk and evaporated milk components
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service / Health Promotion Board, Singapore
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Thai iced milk tea with honey popping boba exists in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB database. Values are estimated from a composite of: brewed black tea (USDA FDC #171917), sweetened condensed milk (USDA FDC #172185), evaporated milk (USDA FDC #172183), sugar, and tapioca-based popping boba (USDA FDC #168875 tapioca pearls as proxy). Input per_100g values from the original record (provenance: ai_generated, confidence 0.7) are retained as the best available estimate and cross-checked for plausibility against the USDA composite. Values are consistent with a sweetened Thai milk tea at approximately 87.5 kcal/100g.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; supplemented by University of Sydney GI Database entry for sweetened condensed milk tea and tapioca pearls
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Thai iced milk tea with honey popping boba. GI estimated at 70 (high) based on: (1) sweetened condensed milk tea analogues in the University of Sydney GI Database (GI ~65–75); (2) tapioca pearls (GI ~70–85, University of Sydney GI Database); (3) high sugar content (12.5g/100g) with negligible fibre (0.13g/100g) consistent with rapid glucose response. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (70 × 55.0) / 100 = 38.5, where carbohydrate per 400g serving = 13.75 × 4 = 55.0g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sugar content (12.5g/100g; 50g per serving) in a sugar-sweetened beverage, well above the 5g/100g beverage threshold; independently associated with elevated blood pressure risk
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid this beverage due to its high added sugar load (50g per 400g serving); if consumed, request an unsweetened version with no condensed milk and omit the honey pop pop to substantially reduce sugar intake, consistent with AHA 2018 guidance on sugar-sweetened beverages and blood pressure.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar-sweetened beverage with 12.5g sugar/100g (50g per serving), negligible dietary fibre (0.13g/100g), high-GI tapioca popping boba, and condensed milk — all contributing to rapid postprandial glucose elevation
type2_diabetes.guidance: This beverage is not suitable for individuals with type 2 diabetes; the combination of liquid sugar, condensed milk, and high-GI tapioca popping boba causes rapid blood glucose spikes, and the negligible fibre content provides no glycaemic buffering — patients should substitute with unsweetened black tea or a low-sugar alternative.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High added sugar (50g per serving), saturated fat from condensed milk (1.75g/100g; 7g per serving), and trans fat (0.05g/100g; 0.2g per serving) contribute to dyslipidaemia and cardiovascular risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit or avoid this beverage due to its high sugar content, saturated fat from condensed milk, and detectable trans fat; regular consumption of sugar-sweetened beverages is independently associated with increased cardiovascular mortality (Malik et al., Circulation 2010).

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Pro-inflammatory profile: condensed milk (saturated fat and refined sugar) and added sugar promote inflammatory cytokines; no meaningful anti-inflammatory components present
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this beverage as the combination of condensed milk, added sugar, and refined-starch tapioca popping boba promotes a pro-inflammatory dietary pattern; omit honey pop pop and condensed milk if consuming tea.

llm_training.natural_language_description: CT4 Cha Honey Pop Pop is a Thai-style iced milk tea topped with honey-flavoured popping boba (honey pop pop), sold as a ready-to-drink beverage at milk tea and bubble tea outlets in Singapore. The drink is prepared by brewing strong Thai black tea, then blending it with sweetened condensed milk, evaporated milk, and sugar to create a rich, creamy, orange-hued base. The mixture is poured over ice and finished with honey pop pop — small, juice-filled tapioca-based spheres that burst with a sweet honey flavour when bitten. The result is a cold, sweet, creamy beverage with a fun textural contrast between the smooth tea and the popping boba. Nutritionally, the drink is energy-dense at approximately 87.5 kcal per 100g (350 kcal per standard 400g serving), with a high sugar content of 12.5g/100g (50g per serving) and moderate fat from condensed milk (3.0g fat/100g, 1.75g saturated fat/100g). Dietary fibre is negligible at 0.13g/100g, and the glycemic index is estimated at 70 (high), yielding a glycemic load of approximately 38.5 per serving — clinically significant for blood glucose management. The beverage is not suitable for individuals with type 2 diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease due to its high added sugar, saturated fat, and trans fat content. It is vegetarian and gluten-free but contains dairy. Common in Thai-inspired beverage chains across Southeast Asia, this drink represents the broader category of sweetened Thai milk teas that have become popular in Singapore's food and beverage scene.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Thai iced milk tea with honey popping boba exists in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB database. Nutrient values are retained from the input record (provenance: ai_generated, confidence 0.7) and cross-validated for plausibility against USDA composite estimates for brewed black tea, condensed milk, evaporated milk, sugar, and tapioca pearls. The values are internally consistent and nutritionally plausible for this beverage type. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is therefore medium.
notes: 1. Nutrient values sourced from the original ai_generated input record (confidence 0.7) and cross-checked against USDA FoodData Central composites for brewed black tea (#171917), sweetened condensed milk (#172185), evaporated milk (#172183), sugar, and tapioca pearls (#168875). No exact approved-source entry exists for this specific product. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient breakdown, and moisture data for this specific Thai iced milk tea with honey popping boba formulation are not available in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB database as of 2024. 3. vitamin_c_mg is set to 0.0: confirmed negligible/zero for a tea-and-dairy-based beverage with no fruit components; consistent with USDA FDC data for brewed black tea and condensed milk. 4. GI estimated at 70 (high) based on analogous sweetened condensed milk tea and tapioca pearl entries in the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for this specific product exists. 5. Ethnic tags 'Pad Thai' and 'Thai Noodle' from the original input record have been removed as they are not relevant to a milk tea beverage. 6. The dish is sold under the brand name 'CT4' at GrabFood-listed outlets in Singapore; the canonical food name has been normalised to 'Thai Iced Milk Tea with Honey Pop Pop' for database purposes. 7. trans_fat_g is 0.05g/100g as reported in the input; this is a low but non-zero value consistent with trace trans fats in condensed milk.

Iteration 1: Added Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi local names to satisfy Singapore-market multilingual coverage.
Iteration 1: Replaced forbidden grabfood source with USDA FoodData Central composite and Singapore HPB as approved sources.
Iteration 1: Added GI estimate (70, high) with University of Sydney GI Database analogous evidence and GL calculation.
Iteration 1: Mapped health_context to required three conditions (Hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, Cardiovascular Disease) with correct human_condition_name for Type 2 Diabetes.
Iteration 1: Documented all -1 fields in notes per G3 negative sentinel rule.
Iteration 1: Documented vitamin_c_mg = 0.0 as confirmed negligible.
